title: Learning to Play Again After Complex Trauma
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: For those healing complex trauma, play can feel impossible. Learn why your nervous system resists joy and how to gently rediscover play as a path to healing.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air. On a simple wooden table, a child's forgotten wooden toy rests, bathed in a warm, gentle glow. The scene evokes a sense of quiet safety and the possibility of rediscovering simple, forgotten joys.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing from complex trauma isn't just about processing the pain, but also about rediscovering the parts of you that went dormant ~ the parts that knew how to play. We often treat play as a luxury, a frivolous activity for children, but for the nervous system shaped by trauma, the absence of play is a real loss, a silent symptom of a life lived in a state of high alert. When we’ve spent years, or even decades, with our internal alarms ringing, the silence of safety can feel more deafening than the chaos it replaced, and in that silence, the forgotten language of play waits to be remembered, not as an achievement, but as a homecoming.

The Ghost of Play in a Traumatized System

For a nervous system organized around survival, the concept of play can feel alien, even dangerous. Hypervigilance, the faithful soldier that stood guard for so long, doesn’t simply clock out when the immediate threat is gone; it remains on high alert, scanning the horizon for the next potential danger. This is not a cognitive choice but a deeply embodied state. The world is perceived through a filter of threat, and every interaction, every moment of unstructured time, is assessed for its potential risk. spontaneity, curiosity, and a lack of clear purpose - are interpreted as vulnerabilities. Why would you let your guard down when the world has proven itself to be unsafe? Why would you engage in an activity without a predictable outcome when unpredictability has so often led to pain?

The process back to play, then, is not about forcing ourselves to have fun. It is about creating the conditions, both internally and externally, where the nervous system can begin to stand down. It is about learning to listen to the subtle cues of our own system, to honor its resistance, and to gently, persistently, offer it a different experience of the world. It is, in essence, a process of reparenting the parts of ourselves that were never given the chance to simply be.

The Tyranny of "Fun" and the Wisdom of the Freeze

The command to "have fun" is often experienced as a form of tyranny for the trauma survivor. It carries an implicit judgment, a suggestion that our inability to spontaneously erupt in joy is a personal failing, rather than a predictable outcome of a nervous system under siege. We find ourselves performing fun, a hollow imitation of an emotion we cannot genuinely access, which only serves to reinforce the sense of alienation from ourselves and others. This performance is exhausting, and it deepens the divide between our inner world and our outer presentation.

The Body as the First Playground

The return to play does not begin with a grand gesture or a forced vacation; it begins in the quiet intimacy of our own bodies. It begins with the radical act of paying attention, of turning our awareness inward and simply noticing what is there, without the immediate impulse to fix, change, or judge it. What we find here is the essence of what the philosopher Jiddu Krishnamurti called "observation without the observer" - a way of seeing that is not colored by our past experiences, our beliefs, or our fears. For the trauma survivor, this is a pioneering act. It is a way of reclaiming the body as a safe and trustworthy home, rather than a source of pain and betrayal.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link).

Bear with me on this one.

Micro-Dosing Joy: The Gentle Return to Spontaneity

The path back to a life that includes play is not a linear ascent but a gentle, often meandering, process of re-acquaintance. It is not about forcing ourselves into situations that feel overwhelming, but about "micro-dosing" joy ~ introducing small, manageable moments of pleasure and spontaneity that can be integrated by the nervous system without triggering a defensive response. It is a way of gently expanding our window of tolerance, that zone of arousal where we can feel engaged and alive without being overwhelmed or shut down.

As we begin to incorporate these small moments of play into our lives, we are, in effect, sending a new message to our nervous system. We are telling it that it is safe to be curious, that it is safe to be spontaneous, that it is safe to experience pleasure. Each time we engage in one of these activities, we are casting a vote for a different way of being in the world. We are strengthening the neural pathways that support connection and ease, and weakening the ones that are wired for threat and defense. the brain's striking ability to reorganize itself in response to new experiences. It is a slow, incremental process, but it is the foundation upon which a more playful, expansive life can be built. It is about learning to follow the breadcrumbs of our own delight, trusting that they will lead us back to the wholeness that was always our birthright. This is a core tenet of Internal Family Systems (IFS), the recognition that all our parts have a role, and that we can bring them all into a more harmonious relationship.

The Architecture of a Playful Existence

While the return to play begins with small, internal shifts, it is sustained by creating an external environment that supports this new way of being. That becomes about more than just scheduling "fun" activities; it is about consciously designing a life that has space for unstructured time, for curiosity, and for the kind of gentle, aimless wandering that is the hallmark of a playful spirit. It requires us to challenge the deeply ingrained belief that our worth is tied to our output, and to embrace a new framework in which being is as valuable as doing.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

Ultimately, building a more playful life is an act of deep self-compassion. It is a recognition that we are not machines, designed for constant productivity, but living organisms that require cycles of rest, renewal, and joyful expression in order to thrive. It is about giving ourselves the permission that we may never have received as children ~ the permission to be messy, to be imperfect, to make mistakes, and to engage in activities simply for the delight of them. For it is only when we have replenished our own wells that we have anything of substance to offer the world. A playful life is not a frivolous one; it is a deeply resilient one, capable of meeting the inevitable challenges of existence with a sense of flexibility, creativity, and an unwavering connection to our own vital life force.

From Hypervigilance to Happenstance

The road from a life defined by hypervigilance to one that has space for happenstance is a striking re-wiring of the self. It is a shift from a world perceived as a series of potential threats to one that is seen as a territory of possibility. It is, rather, a recalibration of the nervous system, a return to a state of balance in which we can accurately assess risk without being held captive by it. It is the difference between a system that is constantly screaming "danger!" and one that is able to whisper, "I am safe enough, right now, in this moment."

I want to end with something that might sound strange. Gratitude for your symptoms. Not toxic positivity gratitude. Not "everything happens for a reason" gratitude. Real gratitude. For the parts of you that kept you alive. For the hypervigilance that spotted real danger. For the dissociation that protected you from unbearable pain. For the fawn response that kept you safe when fighting back wasn't an option.

Richard Schwartz says that when we approach our protector parts with gratitude instead of frustration, something shifts. They soften. They don't need to work so hard. Because they're finally being seen for what they are - not enemies, not pathology, but loyal soldiers who never got the message that the war is over.

So here's my invitation. The next time a symptom shows up - the anxiety, the numbness, the rage, the people-pleasing - before you try to fix it or fight it or shame yourself for it, try this. Say "thank you." Say "I know you're trying to help." Say "I see you." And then, gently, ask: "Is there something else you need me to know?" You might be surprised by the answer. You might be surprised by what becomes possible when you stop fighting yourself and start listening instead.

In my own road, and in the journeys of those I have had the privilege to walk alongside, I have seen that this shift is not a one-time event, but an ongoing process of returning, again and again, to the present moment. It is a practice of noticing when we have been pulled back into the old story of threat and fear, and gently, without judgment, guiding ourselves back to the felt sense of safety in our own skin. The goal is not to eliminate the scars of our past, but to integrate them into a larger, more resilient whole, a self that is capable of holding both the sorrow and the joy, the wound and the wisdom. What emerges is the true meaning of healing - not a return to a state that existed before the trauma, but an arrival into a new, more expansive version of ourselves, one that has room for the full, glorious, and often messy spectrum of human experience.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does trying to have fun make me feel so anxious?

That becomes a common experience for trauma survivors. Your nervous system has been wired to prioritize safety above all else, and activities that are spontaneous or lack a clear purpose can be interpreted as dangerous. The key is to start with micro-doses of pleasure in a way that feels safe and manageable for your system.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Stay with me here.

What if I don't know what I find playful or fun anymore?

It is completely normal to lose touch with your sense of play after years of living in survival mode. The first step is not to force it, but to cultivate curiosity. Think back to your childhood ~ what did you love to do before the world told you who you should be? Is it the color of a flower, the sound of a piece of music, the feeling of a soft blanket? Follow those small glimmers of interest without any expectation; they are the breadcrumbs that will lead you back to your own unique sense of play.

How can I play when I still have so much healing to do?

Play is not a reward for being "healed"; it is an essential part of the healing process itself. Play, even in its smallest forms, is a powerful way to regulate your nervous system and build your capacity for resilience. Think of it not as an "either/or" but a "both/and"... you can hold the grief and the pain of your past while also making space for the lightness and joy of the present.

The road back to play is, in many ways, the process back to the essence of who we are beneath the layers of trauma and conditioning. It is a tender and courageous act of self-reclamation. It is the quiet, steady work of remembering that we are not just our wounds; we are also the boundless, creative, and joyful beings that we were always meant to be.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link).

The only question left is whether today is the day.
